games.explanation: The market is pricing this like the Yankees are the obvious side, and Cam Schlittler is the reason that math doesn't work. He's a righty who walks too many hitters and gets squared up by right-handed bats in particular, which is exactly the half of the Boston lineup the Yankees should be most worried about. Contreras has been mashing all year and is the kind of bat that turns a 3-1 count into a crooked number, and Suarez quietly carries a sub-.290 xwOBA into a park where the wind is blowing across, not out. At +145 on the better-projected team in a coin-flip game, the price is doing the work.

Pricing the Marlins as a coin flip assumes Griffin Jax is the same pitcher against everyone, and he simply isn't. Lefties have torched him to the tune of a .314 xwOBA while righties get the buzzsaw, and Miami happens to roll out a lineup that leans the right direction with Hicks, Stowers, and Edwards stacked at the top. Alcantara isn't the bat-misser he used to be, but he doesn't need to be in a roof-closed game against a Tampa lineup that projects to scratch out under five. At -102 on the better team by a real margin, the board is basically giving you a pick'em on a side that shouldn't be one.

The market is treating this like a coin flip because Burrows looks like a back-end starter on paper, but the Athletics aren't built to make him pay for the mistakes he gives up. Houston is the better team by a wide margin in every direction that matters, with a lineup that runs Alvarez and Paredes against a staff that doesn't have the arms to navigate them. Yordan in particular has been living in the barrel zone against righties, posting a .371 xwOBA that turns any hanging breaker into a crooked number. Oakland's one real threat is Kurtz, and one bat isn't enough to outrun the gap between these rosters. At -108 on the clearly superior side, you're getting a near-coin-flip price on a game that isn't one.

Getting plus money on the better team is the kind of math the market keeps refusing to do, and Cleveland walks in tonight as exactly that. deGrom is brilliant but he is also a contact-suppression arm facing a lineup that just refuses to strike out, and José Ramírez running an 11% K rate against righties is the specific kind of problem deGrom does not enjoy solving. Texas counters with a Rangers offense that has been streaky all year, and Cleveland's bullpen has quietly been one of the steadier units in the league. At +125 on the higher-rated team in a projected one-run game, you take the points and let the dog bark.
